story:
I first see him stagger through the junk, shoved along by a Collector. The boots are too heavy, the gear too stiff. He keeps groping at his face mask with clumsy gloves, lucky he can't undo it. Just before my sector he missteps and goes face-first into a rusted 20th-century car. If the suit's punctured, he's done. The Collector kicks him hard, five quick blows, then dumps him on me. "Recycler, this is X-5783. Instruct him." Then he walks off, light and practiced, well-fed.

"X, huh?" I say. No answer, eyes down. I explain the work: we dig the trash mountain for whatever still has value—copper, intact jars, gold if you're blessed—and the rarest prize of all: sealed food. Find food and you're golden, at least for us Gens. He finally speaks, voice hoarse and younger than I thought. "Have you ever found food?" I tell him yes, once: baby food—Peach and Banana—so bright I just stared at it. He fiddles at his mask again.

"Don't do that," I say. "This air will kill you."

"I can't breathe," he whispers.

"The feeling passes," I tell him, and haul him up.

It's a good day for finds: copper, three jars with lids, an old motherboard. A glint under a pile draws me, but there's a heavy iron pole on top. I ask him to help lift. He doesn't. The cart is filling—mine, not his. No rations without finds. He just trembles. Then I see it: he's crying.

"You're from the Shelter," I say.

He nods. Expelled. He's never seen the outside. I try to imagine it through his eyes: collapsed buildings, rivers of trash, the sky a low ceiling of thick gray, the blue beyond just a handed-down myth—Pepsi-cap blue.

We trade terms. He calls it decontamination; I call it scavenging. I tell him I'm a Gen, born out here because my grandparents were found guilty in the Trials—profited off climate destabilization, not low enough to plead ignorance, not high enough to skate. I talk about assigned sectors, double rations, stretching during Coffin time so the suit doesn't seize my hands.

"No gear in the Shelter?" I ask.

He says no—he worked in food sorting—

Then the Wall Wind hits. You hear it before you feel it. I drop and ball up. He stands, tries to run to me, steps blind over the shifting garbage, and vanishes. I inch forward, spot his arm, drag him out, and steer us back. We keep digging for the shine. Plastic snarls around things that used to live. Carcasses are worst—soft, damp, preserved by plastic and lack of bacteria.

At the bottom of our hole I feel metal. X yells, "That's not yours!" I scramble out in time to see three gray-clad pillagers running off with my cart. We lurch after them, but our boots aren't made for speed. X goes down, spread-eagle. He hasn't tripped—his oxygen line has popped loose. I snap it back. He coughs awake. Dusk deepens.

He asks about the carcasses. I tell him about the old floods—the seas brought the trash and the birds and the fish, then the water left, and everything stayed.

He sighs. "I was expelled for stealing apricot jam. I knew it was wrong. We share everything. But I wanted it all." The clouds turn purple. Objects become shadows of a forgotten past. Back in the airlock, we finally strip off our helmets and I show him the tin. Strawberries. We open it and share, maybe the last on earth, sweet and bright against the gray.
